findings.title: `SessionWorker::new` is still called with the removed `ratelimit_tx` argument
findings.description: This diff removes the `ratelimit_tx` field and constructor parameter from `SessionWorker`, but `SessionManager::get_or_create` still passes `self.ratelimit_tx.clone()` in that call. The next argument (`sessions_clone`) is therefore shifted into the wrong position, so the crate no longer type-checks. This is a hard build break, not a runtime edge case.
findings.suggestion: Update the `SessionWorker::new(...)` call site to match the new signature exactly, or restore the removed constructor parameter and field if rate-limit propagation is still required.

findings.title: Reply text is still injected as privileged prompt content
findings.description: The patch only replaces literal `<system-reminder>` delimiters before embedding reply text inside a bot-authored `<system-reminder>` block. That does not restore the trust boundary: attacker-controlled reply content still arrives wrapped as higher-trust context, so instructions inside the replied-to message can influence the model as if the bot supplied them. Preventing one exact closing tag is not enough for a tool-using bot.
findings.suggestion: Do not place untrusted reply text inside a privileged wrapper. Send it as quoted user content or a separate structured field, and escape/serialize author/content purely for display rather than relying on tag replacement.

findings.title: Rate-limit presence and alerts stop updating because `RateLimit` events are now dropped
findings.description: This diff removes the worker's `ratelimit_tx` plumbing and now explicitly skips `StreamEvent::RateLimit` in the active-turn path. `main.rs` still creates a watch channel and background monitor that derive Discord presence and threshold alerts from that shared `RateLimitInfo`, so after this change those values will stay stale or forever default even when the subprocess emits fresh rate-limit events.
findings.suggestion: Keep forwarding `RateLimit` events into the shared watch channel, or remove the dead monitor/presence wiring in the same change. If the monitor is still intended to work, restore the `send_modify` updates in every path that can read `RateLimit` events.

findings.title: `execute_in_session` arms `needs_context` but never consumes it on that path
findings.description: The new `/new` handling now inserts `thread_id` into `needs_context` from `execute_in_session`, but unlike `handle_message` this function never removes that flag or calls `build_context_content(...)` on later messages. A `/new` sent through the skill-command path therefore leaves stale state behind: subsequent `execute_in_session` calls still send raw content, and the flag survives until some later normal message consumes it unexpectedly.
findings.suggestion: Mirror the same consume-and-inject logic used in `handle_message`, or move the whole `needs_context` state machine into one shared helper/session-layer path so both entry points behave identically.

summary: The diff has one build-breaking constructor mismatch, one remaining prompt-injection boundary issue, and two behavioral regressions around rate-limit propagation and `/new` context state.
